author: kevaundray
content:
Hey, thanks for the interest!

Currently we require only a single block to be proven at once. There is a similar idea known as sub-blocks which we are experimenting with, that allows one to segment a particular block into small blocks and prove those in parallel, it might be of interest to you 🫡

author: xrs_01
content: That sounds very interesting, could you please link me to that proposal? I wasn't aware there was anything similiar. I actually use the term sub-blocks in my own research post!

author: xrs_01
content: https://ethresear.ch/t/why-homogenizing-the-execution-of-the-world-computer-beats-scaling-through-fragmentation/24860 Here's my own post if you're interested!

author: xrs_01
content: Thank you again

author: xrs_01
content: I think the idea of proving in a tree structure is particularly cool because it lets you have throughput with only logarithmic overhead

author: kevaundray
content: I think Ignacio may have a write-up somewhere -- you can also check succincts proof of concept here: https://github.com/succinctlabs/rsp-subblock?ref=blog.succinct.xyz

author: xrs_01
content: Thank you for sending that on. That's a similar idea but the key difference is that in my case the sub-blocks are split across multiple validators, which means really the sub-blocks can be as large as whole blocks. The idea is that you can execute multiple blocks at once and recursively prove them to effectively "shard the execution".

author: xrs_01
content: Basically the bottleneck is the time it takes to execute one branch of the tree, which for a hexary tree with 10s of thousands of blocks being executed at once is only about 4 sequential snark proofs; the rest of the tree's throughput you get for free

author: xrs_01
content: If I'm right, and the networking layer or the parallelization doesn't get in the way, you can potentially reach 100,000 TPS+ with stateless verification

author: xrs_01
content: The nice part about it though is that if the network is asynchronous or otherwise really unhealthy, you can just prune parts of the tree, so the chain always has liveness

author: xrs_01
content: I haven't interacted with Ignacio but do you think we could please include him in this convesation?

author: xrs_01
content: I'm new to research but I'm really excited about this idea and haven't been able to find many people to talk to about it

author: kevaundray
content: Ah he is <@774033563732541451> on discord

author: kevaundray
content: He may be a bit busy, so may not reply soon 😄

author: kevaundray
content: I think one assumption that this seems to make is that any validator can create a zkVM proof for a subblock?

author: xrs_01
content: No worries thank you

author: kevaundray
content: It may also be fruitful to say why the proof size is not a concern re network bandwidth

author: xrs_01
content: Yes the assumption is that the capability is there; the idea though is that in a mono-block model they would instantaneously have to have it during any given slot, since they can always randomly be the proposer. In this case they actually just get the opportunity to contribute every slot

author: kevaundray
content: In the mono-block model, I think a proposer could delegate this to the builder?

author: xrs_01
content: The networking bottleneck is also only as high as it is along any given branch; one nice thing though is that every validator in the tree knows there neighbouring nodes upwards and downwards, so they can direct any data directly in an efficient manner

author: xrs_01
content: in ePBS that will be the case and it will be enshrined, yes. It still works though if you switch the semantics to being "the builders are delegated the sub-blocks" rather than the validators; in this case it's multiple builders executing in parallel as opposed to multiple validating nodes

author: xrs_01
content: The system does still work with ePBS

author: kevaundray
content: Okay I see, would a builder need to be paid for creating subblocks for a block that is not their own in this model?

author: xrs_01
content: The block in a way still is "their own", it's just a fraction of a larger share; so yes they still get a reward for their sub-blocks.

author: xrs_01
content: You basically would be splitting the reward across the participating parties

author: kevaundray
content: Can you say what happens if one of the builders in charge of making a proof for one of the leaf disappears?

author: kevaundray
content: I'm not yet convinced that networking is not an issue 😄

author: xrs_01
content: It's actually execution committees that execute blocks, not individual builders, so there's a degree of redundancy. I call it a "Dexary tree", which means delegated execution N-ary tree. In a hexary tree there's 16 builders in one execution committee, so all 16 would have to fail for them to miss their block. There's also 16 in the "aggregation committee" above. To answer your question, if they do end up missing their sub-block, same as if a slot is missed by a proposer, the nodes just move on and keep building the tree upwards

author: xrs_01
content: It's the same reason why on the consensus layer BLS aggregation isn't a networking problem: the fact that committees are established means you have localized networking environments running in parallel. You basically establish a really efficient path through the network for distributing data since you know the structure of the committees in the tree

author: kevaundray
content:
Ah I was assuming that the execution committee would be builders since they need GPUs in order to do it.

I believe one way to view the idea is that there is a building phase and a proving phase. 

In the building phase, we can do two things:

- Build a block and then split that block into N sub-blocks 
- Allow the capability to build N blocks in parallel such that they do not touch the same state

Once building is done, then the proof generation part would either:

- recursively prove the N sub-blocks. This is possible because the sub-blocks in this case came from a single block and that block was split appropriately. The difference here is that if one of the components is missing, then the whole block is invalid.

- recursively prove the N sequential blocks. Assuming we somehow have N sequential blocks, then if one of the blocks fails, I think you are arguing that this is somehow okay since the blocks are in some sense independent.

If this sounds correct, then I think in the second example, it seems like we do not have a state root that summarises the state of the chain after applying those N blocks?

author: kevaundray
content: I think for BLS the data is small so its okay, though even with that there can be a lot of duplication and wasted bandwidth. This may become a lot better with new research avenues like ethpp2p though

author: xrs_01
content:
Point one:
My idea was that the building and proving is happening at the same point, by the same committees; the element of parallelism is true that you're making a best-effort to split them up to be parallelizable, but I also proposed having aggregation committees solve any dependencies between state changes at higher layers. 

Point two:
If one sub-block is invalid, it doesn't invalidate the entire larger block. Since the proofs are recursively aggregated, the state-changes of the sub-block just wouldn't have taken place. In other words, by the time the higher layers started building their recursive proofs, they wouldn't have the sub-block available, so wouldn't include it in their proofs.

author: xrs_01
content: It is a localized thing though, and if the networking does start to fail, you just lose a small part of the execution

author: xrs_01
content: It's similar to BLS in that way; if you lose a committe's attestations, they just aren't included, it doesn't break anything else

author: xrs_01
content: The closest comparison I can give is actually the Lean Multisig

author: xrs_01
content: I do hope the concept is coming across because I think it is very cool, and should be explored even on a minimal level

author: xrs_01
content: I think the idea that at any one time only one validator should be executing is a huge oversight. I feel like you cannot scale a world-computer by executing the entire universe on one thread.

author: xrs_01
content: If you even did something basic like there are 16 builders per block and their proofs are aggregated into one you could get a large margin of scaling even without the tree-structure
